title: Dog Having Two Seizures in One Hour: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Two seizures in one hour (cluster seizures) is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn why immediate care is needed and how to help your dog now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Multiple seizures within a 24-hour period are a critical medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention. Seek emergency care right away to prevent life-threatening complications like status epilepticus or severe overheating.

What are cluster seizures and what does it mean if my dog has two seizures in one hour?

When a dog experiences two or more seizures within a 24-hour period, it is medically referred to as cluster seizures. Experiencing two seizures within a single hour is a particularly intense and concerning neurological event. This indicates that the brain's electrical activity is significantly disrupted and the body is struggling to return to a normal state between episodes.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog has multiple seizures in a short time?

  • Urgency Level: High. Two seizures in one hour is considered a critical medical emergency.
  • Health Risks: Frequent seizures can lead to hyperthermia (a dangerous spike in body temperature) and may progress into status epilepticus.
  • Life-Threatening State: Status epilepticus is a condition where the brain remains in a continuous seizure, which can be fatal.
  • Immediate Action: Contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately to stabilize your dog and break the seizure cycle.

How does recording a video of the seizure help my veterinarian with triage?

  • Visual Evidence: A brief video allows the doctor to observe specific muscle movements and the exact duration of the event.
  • Assessment: It helps the veterinary team evaluate your dog's level of consciousness during the episode.
  • Differential Diagnosis: Visuals help distinguish neurological seizures from other conditions like fainting or tremors.
  • Targeted Treatment: Accurate documentation ensures your dog receives the correct medication as soon as you arrive at the clinic.
  • Safety First: Only capture video if it is safe to do so or if a second person is available to assist.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of more than one seizure of unknown cause in a single day, maintenance antiepileptic drug (AED) therapy should be considered. For persistent status epilepticus, diazepam may be administered as a constant-rate infusion at 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr. If phenobarbital is contraindicated due to preexisting hepatic conditions, levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg may be administered intravenously, subcutaneously, or rectally. Oral anticonvulsants should be resumed as soon as possible if the animal is currently receiving them.

Chapter: Neurology, Pharmacology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2596)

faqs.question: What should I do while my dog is seizing?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog clear of stairs or sharp furniture. Do not put your hands or any objects in their mouth, as they may accidentally bite. Remain calm and speak in a soothing voice until the episode passes.

faqs.question: Can I wait until my regular vet opens?
faqs.answer: No. Cluster seizures are a progressive emergency. Waiting several hours increases the risk of permanent brain damage or life-threatening complications. Seek an emergency clinic immediately.

faqs.question: Will my dog be okay after the seizures stop?
faqs.answer: While many dogs recover from individual episodes, the underlying cause of cluster seizures must be identified and managed by a veterinarian to prevent future, more severe occurrences.
